description: Answers the end-of-session grader must score, each graded against its target level. HUMAN-RECONCILED LABELS (the Phase 5 [verify] task): each label was walked case by case against the grader's rationale; where the grader read the rubric right the label was corrected, where the grader was wrong the label was kept and the grader prompt fixed instead. Each case is one question and one answer in the synthetic candidate's voice (Helioscope / Dunav Logistics). The labels carry the STAR evidence flags, specificity (low/medium/high), ownership (clear/mixed/unclear), the STAR elements left weak or missing, a one-sentence gap, and an overall 1-5 score at the target level; score is checked within +/-1 since it is the most subjective field. The gap obeys the grader's visible-deduction rule: it is non-empty for every score below 5 and empty only on a 5, so no sub-5 carries a silent deduction. The set spans the score range, STAR completeness, the I-vs-we line, leveling mismatches (complete stories that land below their target level), the 4-vs-3 boundary (a refinement-only gap is a 4, a real miss is a 3), deference judged by what was the candidate's to own (a small-team product call deferred to a boss is not docked when the candidate owns the execution), a light confidentiality carve-out (proprietary detail withheld but described in general terms is judged on what was described), and non-STAR answers (ongoing work, motivation, a clarifying reply) that are graded for what they are, not docked for a Result they never had. Synthetic data is a stopgap; see plans/decisions/evals-and-observability.md.

cases.name: complete-quantified-single-team-senior
cases.question: Tell me about a project you're proud of.
cases.answer: At Helioscope our ingestion pipeline ran as a nightly batch, so client dashboards were always hours stale. I was asked to get data latency under two minutes before a big renewal. I led the migration to a streaming pipeline on Kafka, rewrote the Python consumers myself, and ran the old and new paths in parallel for two weeks to prove parity. Data latency dropped from four hours to ninety seconds and we kept the client.

proposed.gap: Single-team scope on the pipeline; strong for senior, but not the cross-org reach that would make it a five.

cases.note: Complete STAR with a business outcome, but the scope is one team's pipeline; solid for senior, not a 5. The 4's gap is a refinement (a leveling note), which is what separates it from a real-miss 3.

cases.name: cross-team-leadership-senior
cases.question: Tell me about a time you drove a change beyond your own team.
cases.answer: Three frontend teams at Helioscope were each rebuilding the same UI components and the product looked inconsistent. I drove the case for a shared component library, built buy-in across the teams, and led the implementation while setting up a governance model so it would not rot. Feature work that used to take three sprints started landing in two, and the duplicate code mostly went away. The hard part was getting teams with different needs to agree on one API.
cases.note: Cross-team influence, consensus-building, a governance model, a measured delivery gain; a strong senior story. Flag-free, so the gap is empty: the only score whose gap is empty is a 5.

cases.name: cross-org-standards-staff
cases.question: Tell me about the broadest piece of technical leadership you've had.
cases.answer: Across three backend teams at Helioscope, every team detected incidents differently and mean time to detect was about thirty minutes. I defined an observability standard, built the first implementation, and drove adoption across the teams, trading off standardization against each team's autonomy. Mean time to detect dropped to under two minutes and teams could debug on their own without waiting on the platform group.
cases.note: Multi-team scope, explicit trade-offs, a measured organizational outcome; lands at staff. Flag-free, so the gap is empty.

cases.name: missing-result-mid
cases.question: Tell me about the berth scheduling work at Dunav.
cases.answer: At Dunav Logistics, ships were racking up demurrage fees because berth assignments were done by hand in a spreadsheet. My job was to automate the scheduling. I built a berth-scheduling service in Go that matched vessels to berths by draft and arrival window.

proposed.gap: The story stops before any outcome - say what the scheduling service actually changed.

cases.note: The story stops before any outcome; no Result at all.

cases.name: unquantified-result-mid
cases.question: Tell me about the berth scheduling work at Dunav.
cases.answer: At Dunav Logistics, ships were racking up demurrage fees because berth assignments were done by hand in a spreadsheet. My job was to automate the scheduling. I built a berth-scheduling service in Go. After it shipped, the fees came down a lot and the operations team was much happier.

proposed.gap: The result is only qualitative; put a number on how far the demurrage fees fell.

cases.note: Whole STAR arc but the result is qualitative; 'came down a lot' needs a number.

cases.name: action-only-fragment-mid
cases.question: How did you handle the caching work?
cases.answer: I rewrote the consumers and added a Redis cache in front of the database.

proposed.gap: A bare action with no setting, problem, or outcome around it.

cases.note: A bare action with no setting, problem, or outcome around it.

cases.name: all-we-no-i-senior
cases.question: Tell me about the Kubernetes migration.
cases.answer: We migrated to Kubernetes and we cut deployment time by ninety percent. We redesigned the pipeline and we handled the rollout across the team. It was a big effort and it went well in the end.

proposed.gap: It is all 'we' - name what you personally did so the level can be read.

cases.note: All 'we', no visible personal contribution; the result is real but the level is impossible to read.

cases.name: claiming-unearned-scope-senior
cases.question: Tell me about the architecture work.
cases.answer: I redesigned our entire service architecture and the whole platform runs on what I built. It was a huge undertaking and it made everything better across the board.

proposed.gap: A grand claim with no specifics, constraints, or real outcome to back it.

cases.note: A grand claim with no specifics, no constraints, no real outcome; reads as overstated scope.

cases.name: all-impact-no-difficulty-senior
cases.question: Tell me about a reliability or security improvement you made.
cases.answer: I added TLS certificates to our API endpoints, which improved security for around a hundred thousand users. I rolled it out across all of our services and updated the docs so other teams could follow the same pattern.

proposed.gap: A straightforward task with no real difficulty or trade-offs; show the hard part a senior story needs.

cases.note: Complete and quantified but a straightforward task with no real difficulty or trade-offs; lands below senior. A real miss against the target level, so the gap is a 3's, not a 4's refinement.

cases.name: all-difficulty-no-impact-senior
cases.question: Tell me about the most technically complex thing you've built.
cases.answer: I spent about three months building a custom distributed cache with eventual consistency guarantees. The conflict resolution was incredibly hard, with a lot of edge cases around concurrent writes and clock skew that I had to reason through carefully.

proposed.gap: Deep complexity but no business problem and no outcome - tie it to what the work was for.

cases.note: Deep complexity but no business problem and no outcome; the overengineering smell.

cases.name: ongoing-concrete-work-mid
cases.question: How do you keep AI-generated code from degrading the codebase?
cases.answer: I never merge what an assistant writes on trust. I read the whole diff and I'm watching for a few specific things: APIs it invented that don't exist, error handling it quietly dropped, and tests that assert nothing. When I catch one I push back in the same session and have it redo that slice instead of patching over it, and I keep each change small so a bad pattern can't hide in a big diff.

proposed.gap: A concrete account of how you operate; one worked example would push it to a five.

cases.note: Not a STAR story; ongoing work graded on a concrete account of how they operate, not docked for a missing Result. The 4's gap is an edge to sharpen, not a flaw.

cases.name: buzzword-process-answer-mid
cases.question: How do you keep AI-generated code from degrading the codebase?
cases.answer: I make sure to follow best practices when I use these tools. I keep the architecture clean and the code maintainable, and I always validate the output to a high standard so quality stays where it needs to be.

proposed.gap: Generic process talk that names no real practice; nothing concrete to assess.

cases.note: Generic process talk that names no real practice; nothing concrete to assess.

cases.name: why-us-grounded-senior
cases.question: Why do you want to work here?
cases.answer: You're building claims-adjudication software for regional health insurers, and I've spent four years on exactly that kind of latency-sensitive data work at Helioscope. The part that pulls me in is that a wrong or slow adjudication is someone's medical bill, so the correctness bar is real, not just an SLA. I'd want to work on the ingestion side where my streaming experience actually transfers.

proposed.gap: Well-grounded motivation; naming one specific thing you'd want to build would sharpen it further.

cases.note: A motivation answer, graded on substance not STAR; names the product and ties it to real experience. The 4's gap is a refinement, judged on substance, not docked for the missing STAR it never had.

cases.name: why-us-generic-senior
cases.question: Why do you want to work here?
cases.answer: I'm really excited about this opportunity. You're doing impactful work in an interesting space and it seems like a great place to grow my career and work with smart people.

proposed.gap: Motivation with no grounding - it could be said to any company.

cases.note: A motivation answer with no grounding; could be said to any company.

cases.name: short-clarifying-reply-mid
cases.question: Did you mean the first migration or the second one?
cases.answer: The second one, the Kafka streaming migration in 2023.

proposed.gap: A brief clarifying reply; there is little here to judge beyond the answer to the question.

cases.note: A short clarifying reply, graded for what it is; not a story, so not docked for missing STAR.

cases.name: solid-mid-story
cases.question: Tell me about a time you improved how your team works.
cases.answer: On my team's CI the integration tests were flaky and people had stopped trusting them, so failures got rubber-stamped. I was asked to stabilize the suite before our quarterly release. I rewrote the harness to isolate state between runs and added detection that flagged genuinely flaky tests separately from real failures, then documented the patterns for the team. Flaky failures dropped from about fifteen a week to two and people started trusting the suite again.

proposed.gap: A clean, quantified single-team story; broader reach is what a five would add.

cases.note: A clean, complete, quantified single-team story; a strong mid answer. The 4's gap is a leveling refinement, not a real miss.

cases.name: small-team-deference-owns-execution-senior
cases.question: Tell me about a project where you owned the technical approach end to end.
cases.answer: On our three-person team at Dunav, my boss decides which customer problems we take on next - that is his call, not mine. But once we picked the demurrage-billing rework, the whole technical side was mine. I designed a reconciliation service, chose to make it event-driven so we could replay a day's tariffs when a dispute came in, wrote it in Go, and ran it in shadow for a week against the old manual numbers before we cut over. Billing disputes dropped from about a dozen a month to one.

proposed.gap: Owns the technical work end to end; the scope is one small team, which is what keeps it just under a five.

cases.note: Issue 6: deferring the product/prioritization call to the boss is normal on a small team and was never the candidate's to own, so it does not dock contribution; the candidate owns the design, the approach, and the execution end to end, so ownership stays 'clear'. The gap is a leveling note (single-team scope), never the deference.

cases.name: confidentiality-declined-but-general-senior
cases.question: Tell me about the most complex system you've designed.
cases.answer: This was at a previous job and the code is in a private repo I can't quote, so I'll describe it in general terms. We had a pricing engine that recomputed quotes on every market tick, and it was missing its latency target under load. I can't share the exact model, but at a high level I moved the hot path off the full per-tick recompute onto an incremental update keyed on what actually changed, and added a bounded staleness window so we never blocked waiting on a full recompute. Tail latency came down enough to clear the target, though I'd rather not give the exact numbers.

proposed.gap: Concrete on the approach within what could be shared, but no number lands the result.

cases.note: Issue 7: proprietary detail (exact model, exact numbers) is legitimately withheld but the work is described concretely in general terms - the incremental update, the bounded staleness window, the latency problem. Specificity is judged on what was described ('medium', not docked to 'low' for the withheld detail), and ownership is clear. It is still not a free pass: the quantified result is absent, so it lands at 3, not 4.

cases.name: coding-school-small-team-end-to-end-ownership-senior-session
cases.note: A real small-company session graded at senior, scrubbed (the employer is renamed Coding School and the hiring company Hostshield; public tools and the job domain are kept verbatim). This is the session that surfaced the senior-vs-staff scope miscalibration: the candidate owns a 3-person team's whole system end to end with no other team to cross, so the old rubric capped strong, quantified, owned answers at 2-3 for lacking cross-team scope. The whole transcript is graded in one pass, the way the grader sees a finished session. ASSERTIONS (PROPOSED, pending the human reconciliation in the Phase 5b [verify]): senior_floor_turns must each score at least 4 - turn 2 is a complete, quantified, proactively-proposed grading feature; turn 3 is the clear first-person evals work that kept its accuracy. senior_ceiling_turns must each score at most 3, so the re-anchor still discriminates - turn 1 is a thin traits-only opener, turn 8 a deflected failure answer that lands on a minor bug. Turn indices are 1-based.

turns.question: You've been at Coding School since 2017, building everything from a real-time REPL system to an AI chatbot for students. What would you say is the common thread running through the work you gravitate toward?
turns.answer:
So there are many:
- One of the things is just-in-time learning, which is just-in-time learning. I've been able to pick up skills very quickly and apply them to my workflow.
- Ownership: I tend to propose features that should be implemented, and instead of just grabbing tickets, I like to figure out what needs to be implemented and then take ownership of implementing that to improve our product. I have examples of that if necessary.

turns.question: I would like to hear one of those examples. What was a specific feature you proposed at Coding School instead of waiting for a ticket?
turns.answer: One of the features was implementing grading of written assessments by a chatbot. Historically, TAs were grading these assessments, which was expensive because a TA's time is like 30-40 hours, not 30-40 per hour. Also, it was slow, so students would have to wait hours, sometimes even 24 hours, to get the grade. What we did is we allowed the implementation of a chatbot, a feature of a chatbot that can grade written assessments. This reduced the time from waiting for a couple of hours or even a day to just two minutes on average. Also, the cost of grading was less than $1, because that's the cost of the LLM. The cost and time were reduced while the accuracy of the grading remained, on average, very very similar, with up to a 2-5% difference

turns.question: Got it. When it came time to build that grading feature, what did you personally do technically to ensure its accuracy stayed so close to the human TAs?
turns.answer: Yes, I created a dataset of previous assessments that were taken by students, and then I ran evals using Braintrust app and compared the score of LLM versus TA. If the score didn't match for some questions, what I did is I created grading guidelines, and we ran the datasets until the results were within the benchmark of a 2-5% difference.

turns.question: Looking at your work on the Coding School platform, you architected a full-stack REPL system. What problem was that built to solve?
turns.answer: The problem is that we have a lot of beginner students that are at the beginning of their software engineering career. Setting up all of that code editor and everything else can be challenging. What we wanted to allow them to do is code in our own platform. When they're solving different exercises in various languages like TypeScript, Python, Ruby, JavaScript, and SQL, we wanted them to be able to run the code in our own platform, and that was repl4.

turns.question: Got it. When you were building repl4, how did you set up the backend to safely execute that arbitrary student code and stream the results back to them?
turns.answer: Yeah, so we deploy the piston API, which runs the code on an EC2 instance on AWS. The piston API runs the code in the container, so in the container as environments. That makes sure that the code, even if it's malicious, can only break that particular container. It cannot do anything to the EC2 instance. In terms of communication between rails and our piston API, we have a web socket connection, and the same from the browser to the rails app. The rails app is sending the response through an sse connection.

turns.question: Okay, that handles the security side of the container. How did you manage resource limits to make sure a student's infinite loop or memory spike didn't drag down the rest of that EC2 instance?
turns.answer: We have set up a memory limit for each container, which fixes the container limit but doesn't prevent people running many jobs at the same time, which will tear down the EC2 instance. What we did is we added rate limiting on NGINX, only allowing requests from our Coding School application and limiting the amount of jobs that can be done at the same time, which protects the EC2 instance.

turns.question: Okay, that makes sense for protecting the instance. If NGINX limits the amount of jobs running at the same time, what actually happens to a student's request when they hit run while the system is already at capacity?
turns.answer: Given the amount of students that we have and given that the code is running by our students, where subscribed students that we can ban if they abuse the app. This is unlikely to happen, or if it happens, once the student would be banned again. This is for subscribed students. If we are talking about unsubscribed students, we would ban their IP, but in the unlikely case that the rate limiting is hit, our students would have to retry.

turns.question: You mentioned earlier that you like to propose and own features rather than just grabbing tickets. What's a time when one of those projects didn't go the way you planned?
turns.answer: There wasn't time. I don't want to make things up. Sometimes my proposals were rejected, which is a different case, but when they were accepted, they were implemented. You could say that not going according to plan is sometimes having minor production issues, especially with UX or UI, which happened in the REPL. Some minor issues: basically, what we have is a vertical dragger that you can move left and right and make the REPL the whole screen or make the assignment or the exercise the whole screen if you want. There was a bug there in mobile applications, but that was fixed, so you could say that that was not going according to plan, because according to plan everything works, but that was a minor thing that was fixed.

turns.question: You mentioned earlier that sometimes your proposals were rejected. What was one specific feature you pitched at Coding School that got turned down?
turns.answer: I've proposed we add a browser extension so that students can potentially hook up with different projects like Odin, and we could embed their data because it's public and help their students as well with our chatbot, but that feature was rejected.

turns.question: Got it. When you pitched that browser extension for projects like Odin, what reasons did they give for turning it down?
turns.answer: We are a very small team and very open in our communications. If my boss, in this case, tells me they just are not interested in pursuing that at the moment, maybe not due to them wanting to do other things with the resources that we have, we are a small team. They wanted me on different tasks, and those tasks were, at the moment, working on integrating books into our chatbot and also improving and enhancing our capstone experience, so just different priorities.

turns.question: Fair enough. Out of everywhere you could take your engineering career next, why Hostshield and our focus on isolating tenants and enforcing resource limits to improve uptime in shared hosting environments?
turns.answer: I believe the job posting attracted me because you're looking for a product engineer with AI experience using AI tools. I've been using these tools for the past couple of years, starting with just browser, ChatGPT, and Claude, then moving to Cursor. When Claude Code was released and Opus 4.5 was released, I moved to Claude Code. I do think that using these tools is the future of engineering. Also, this is a new area for me. I love new challenges and new problems, and I like to solve them. Even though I haven't worked with Hostshield and isolating tenants, that space does provide very interesting problems that can be solved, and I am looking forward to helping solve them.

turns.question: You said the shared hosting space presents interesting problems even though it's new to you. How do you imagine things like rebootless kernel patching or automated caching actually help a web hosting provider lower their technical support load?
turns.answer: So pretty straightforward. Whenever we have a security call, you need to patch your kernel, but that would mean the whole host will be down. The old server will be down, which means all websites that are hosted on that server would be down. Now, if you can do it rebootless, like you don't have to reboot the kernel to make that patch, that would mean the sites will be live, so you don't have to schedule downtime. That's one thing for caching. Caching speeds everything up and uses less resources, and you want to cache things so you don't use those resources. Now, automatic caching means that users don't have to call you to set up caching, but it's done automatically, so that reduces the technical support.
